_purpose: Community Texture Profile — institutional and social infrastructure of a specific community. Every institution is both a community anchor AND a record-keeping body. Tells the narrative AI what settled life looked like and where to find records of it.

location.geographic_scope: Paradise Valley and Black Bottom, Detroit — roughly bounded by Gratiot Avenue, Brush Street, the Detroit River, and the Grand Trunk railroad tracks. Paradise Valley centered on Hastings Street from Adams to Canfield; Black Bottom was the residential area east of Hastings.

description: The primary destination neighborhood for African Americans arriving in Detroit during the Great Migration. Henry Ford's revolutionary $5-a-day wage — offered without racial distinction on the assembly line — drew tens of thousands of Black Southerners to Detroit beginning in 1914. Racial covenants and white hostility confined them to a small, aging neighborhood east of downtown that Black residents transformed into a thriving community. Paradise Valley became Detroit's Black commercial and entertainment district, while Black Bottom was the dense residential core. At its peak in the 1940s, the neighborhood held over 100,000 people and was the center of Black Detroit's economic, religious, and cultural life. The community was destroyed between 1950 and 1966 by urban renewal and freeway construction — I-375 (the Chrysler Freeway) was routed directly through its heart.

churches_and_religious.name: Second Baptist Church of Detroit
churches_and_religious.founded: 1836 (oldest Black congregation in Michigan; a station on the Underground Railroad)
churches_and_religious.location: 441 Monroe Avenue (original location near Paradise Valley; relocated to current site at 3961 Brush Street in 1914)
churches_and_religious.significance: The foundational institution of Black Detroit. Founded by thirteen formerly enslaved people, Second Baptist served as the last stop on the Underground Railroad before Canada. By the Great Migration era, it was the largest and most prestigious Black church in the city, providing employment assistance, housing referrals, and social services to Southern newcomers.
churches_and_religious.records_available: Membership rolls dating to the 1830s, baptism and marriage records, funeral programs, annual reports. Held by the congregation; some materials at the Burton Historical Collection (Detroit Public Library) and the Bentley Historical Library (University of Michigan).
churches_and_religious.genealogical_note: Second Baptist's membership records are among the oldest Black church records in the Midwest. If your ancestor was in pre-Migration Detroit, start here. For Migration-era arrivals, check whether the church recorded the newcomer's home state and sending church.

churches_and_religious.name: St. Matthew's Episcopal Church
churches_and_religious.significance: The oldest Black Episcopal congregation in Michigan and a center of the Black professional class. St. Matthew's parishioners were disproportionately teachers, doctors, lawyers, and business owners. The church was a pillar of Detroit's 'old settler' community.
churches_and_religious.records_available: Parish registers (baptism, confirmation, marriage, burial), vestry minutes. Held by the congregation and the Episcopal Diocese of Michigan archives.
churches_and_religious.genealogical_note: Episcopal parish registers are unusually detailed — they often include parents' names, places of birth, and sponsors for baptism. If your ancestor was part of Detroit's Black elite, check St. Matthew's records before the Baptist churches.

churches_and_religious.name: Bethel AME Church
churches_and_religious.location: 5050 St. Antoine Street (multiple locations over time)
churches_and_religious.significance: Second-oldest Black congregation in Detroit. A center of community activism from abolition through the civil rights era. Rev. William H. Peck led the congregation during the Great Migration years and was deeply involved in NAACP and Urban League work.
churches_and_religious.records_available: Membership rolls, conference records, women's auxiliary minutes. Held by the congregation; AME denominational records at the AME Church archives.
churches_and_religious.genealogical_note: AME church records track transfers between congregations — if your ancestor moved from a Southern AME church to Bethel, both congregations may hold matching transfer records.

churches_and_religious.name: Hartford Avenue Baptist Church
churches_and_religious.location: Hartford Avenue at Beaubien Street (Black Bottom)
churches_and_religious.significance: Founded during the first wave of the Great Migration by Southern newcomers who found the established churches too formal. Grew rapidly to become one of the largest congregations in Black Bottom, serving working-class auto workers and their families.
churches_and_religious.records_available: Membership rolls, baptism records. Held by the congregation.
churches_and_religious.genealogical_note: Hartford Avenue Baptist drew heavily from Alabama and Georgia migrants. If your ancestor came from those states and was working-class Baptist, this congregation is a strong lead.

churches_and_religious.name: Plymouth Congregational Church (United Church of Christ)
churches_and_religious.significance: Under Rev. Horace White (1936-1958), Plymouth became the intellectual center of Black Detroit — hosting forums, debates, and community organizing. The congregation attracted educators, professionals, and progressive activists.
churches_and_religious.records_available: Membership rolls, church bulletins, event programs. Held by the congregation.
churches_and_religious.genealogical_note: If your ancestor was a teacher, social worker, or professional in 1930s-1950s Detroit, Plymouth Congregational was a likely church home. Cross-reference with Michigan Chronicle social columns.

schools_and_education.name: Miller High School
schools_and_education.location: Rivard Street at Hendrie, Black Bottom
schools_and_education.significance: The primary high school serving Black Bottom. Though technically integrated, the student body was overwhelmingly Black due to residential segregation. Miller's yearbooks document two generations of Black Bottom families.
schools_and_education.records_available: Yearbooks, some enrollment records. Held by the Burton Historical Collection (Detroit Public Library); Detroit Public Schools archives.
schools_and_education.genealogical_note: Miller High School yearbooks from the 1930s-1960s are photographic records of Black Bottom families. The Burton Historical Collection holds a nearly complete set.

schools_and_education.name: Cass Technical High School
schools_and_education.significance: Detroit's premier academic high school, one of the few that was meaningfully integrated. Black students who attended Cass Tech were typically from the aspiring middle class — families investing heavily in their children's education. Notable Black alumni include Diana Ross and Lily Tomlin.
schools_and_education.records_available: Yearbooks, alumni records. Held by the school and Burton Historical Collection.
schools_and_education.genealogical_note: If your ancestor attended Cass Tech rather than the neighborhood school, it signals a family that prioritized education and upward mobility — context that enriches the narrative.

newspapers_and_media.name: The Detroit Tribune
newspapers_and_media.significance: Major Black newspaper covering Paradise Valley and Black Bottom. Published extensive social news, church reports, and community events. Less widely digitized than the Michigan Chronicle, making it an underused genealogical source.
newspapers_and_media.records_available: Microfilm at the Burton Historical Collection (Detroit Public Library). Partial digitization through various databases.
newspapers_and_media.genealogical_note: The Tribune's social columns documented weddings, funerals, church events, and club meetings in granular detail. Search microfilm at the Burton Collection for your ancestor's name.

newspapers_and_media.name: Michigan Chronicle
newspapers_and_media.significance: Founded by John H. Sengstacke (who also owned the Chicago Defender), the Michigan Chronicle became Detroit's newspaper of record for Black life. Covered civil rights, labor politics, UAW integration battles, and community news with depth and consistency.
newspapers_and_media.records_available: Microfilm at the Burton Historical Collection and Detroit Public Library branches. Some issues available through ProQuest and other databases.
newspapers_and_media.genealogical_note: The Michigan Chronicle published detailed obituaries, wedding announcements, and social club reports. For post-1936 research, it is the single richest newspaper source for Black Detroit genealogy.

fraternal_and_mutual_aid.name: Detroit Urban League
fraternal_and_mutual_aid.significance: Founded specifically to assist Great Migration arrivals. Provided employment placement, housing assistance, and acculturation services. The Urban League worked directly with Ford Motor Company and other employers to place Black workers, making it a crucial link between Southern arrivals and industrial employment.
fraternal_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Case files, annual reports, employment placement records. Held by the Bentley Historical Library (University of Michigan) and the Burton Historical Collection.
fraternal_and_mutual_aid.genealogical_note: The Detroit Urban League's case files are an extraordinary genealogical resource — they record the client's name, former address in the South, arrival date in Detroit, family members, skills, and employment placement. If your ancestor arrived between 1916 and 1940, check these files.

fraternal_and_mutual_aid.name: Booker T. Washington Trade Association
fraternal_and_mutual_aid.significance: The central organization of Black business owners in Paradise Valley. Promoted Black entrepreneurship, organized the annual 'Business Week' celebrations, and lobbied for Black economic development. Members included owners of nightclubs, barbershops, funeral homes, hotels, and restaurants along Hastings Street.
fraternal_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Membership directories, meeting minutes. Some materials at the Burton Historical Collection.
fraternal_and_mutual_aid.genealogical_note: If your ancestor owned a business in Paradise Valley, the Booker T. Washington Trade Association membership directory will list them. Cross-reference with Detroit city directories and Michigan Chronicle business advertisements.

fraternal_and_mutual_aid.name: Prince Hall Masonic Lodge (Cornerstone Lodge No. 7 and others)
fraternal_and_mutual_aid.significance: Prince Hall Freemasonry was the most prestigious Black fraternal organization in Detroit. Lodge membership conferred social status and provided sick benefits, death benefits, and a mutual support network. Multiple lodges served different social strata of Black Detroit.
fraternal_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Lodge membership rolls, benefit payment records, annual reports. Held by individual lodges and the Prince Hall Grand Lodge of Michigan.
fraternal_and_mutual_aid.genealogical_note: Masonic records include initiation dates, occupations, addresses, and beneficiaries. If your ancestor was a man active in community life, check Prince Hall lodge records — membership rates among Black men in Detroit were very high.

businesses_and_commerce.name: Great Lakes Mutual Life Insurance Company
businesses_and_commerce.significance: The largest Black-owned insurance company in Michigan. Employed hundreds of Black agents who served as community intermediaries, visiting homes weekly to collect premiums. Insured Black families routinely denied coverage by white companies.
businesses_and_commerce.records_available: Company records; some materials at the Burton Historical Collection. Policy records may survive in state insurance commission archives.
businesses_and_commerce.genealogical_note: Life insurance policies record the insured's age, address, family members listed as beneficiaries, and sometimes cause of death. Great Lakes Mutual served thousands of Black Detroit families.

businesses_and_commerce.name: Hastings Street Businesses (collective entry)
businesses_and_commerce.type: Commercial corridor — nightclubs, barbershops, restaurants, hotels, stores
businesses_and_commerce.location: Hastings Street, from Gratiot Avenue to East Forest Avenue
businesses_and_commerce.significance: Hastings Street was the commercial spine of Paradise Valley — a dense corridor of Black-owned businesses including the Flame Show Bar, the Club Three Sixes, Sportree's Music Bar, the Gotham Hotel, Barthwell's Drug Store, and dozens of barbershops, beauty parlors, and restaurants. It was also the center of Detroit's blues scene, where John Lee Hooker, Bobo Jenkins, and others forged a distinctive electric blues sound.
businesses_and_commerce.records_available: Detroit city directories list businesses by address; advertisements in the Michigan Chronicle and Detroit Tribune; photographs at the Burton Historical Collection.
businesses_and_commerce.genealogical_note: Detroit city directories (available at the Burton Historical Collection and through Ancestry.com) list businesses by street address. Search Hastings Street addresses for your ancestor's business. The Michigan Chronicle's annual 'Progress Edition' featured profiles of Black-owned businesses.

cultural_institutions.name: Brewster-Douglass Housing Projects
cultural_institutions.location: Brewster Street between Beaubien and St. Antoine, east side of Detroit
cultural_institutions.significance: The first federally funded public housing project for African Americans in the United States. When it opened in 1935, Brewster Homes was a vast improvement over the overcrowded, substandard housing in Black Bottom. Residents included a young Diana Ross, Lily Tomlin, and Smokey Robinson. The projects were a community in themselves — with tenant councils, recreation programs, and social organizations.
cultural_institutions.records_available: Detroit Housing Commission records, tenant applications, tenant council minutes. Held at the Burton Historical Collection and the National Archives (Great Lakes Region).
cultural_institutions.genealogical_note: Public housing applications required detailed family information — names, ages, prior addresses, income, employment. If your ancestor lived in the Brewster Projects, these applications are a rich genealogical source. Check the National Archives Great Lakes Region (Chicago) and the Burton Historical Collection.

cultural_institutions.name: Your Heritage House
cultural_institutions.type: Cultural and educational center for children
cultural_institutions.era: 1969-present (founded in response to Paradise Valley's destruction)
cultural_institutions.significance: Founded by Dr. Charles H. Wright (who also founded the Museum of African American History) to preserve the cultural heritage that urban renewal had physically destroyed. While post-Paradise Valley, it represents the community's effort to maintain institutional memory.
cultural_institutions.records_available: Organizational records, oral history collections. Some materials at the Charles H. Wright Museum.
cultural_institutions.genealogical_note: Your Heritage House and the Charles H. Wright Museum of African American History (315 East Warren Avenue) hold oral history collections from former Paradise Valley and Black Bottom residents — primary sources for families displaced by urban renewal.

social_fabric.community_structure: Paradise Valley was organized around three interlocking systems: the automobile industry (which sorted families by employer — Ford, Chrysler, Briggs — and by UAW local), the Black church (which sorted families by denomination, class, and Southern origin), and the hustling economy of Hastings Street (numbers running, nightclubs, after-hours joints). The typical migration chain worked through kin and hometown networks: a man already working at Ford's Rouge plant would write home to Alabama or Mississippi, a relative would come up, stay in the newcomer's apartment until finding work, then send for his own family. The Detroit Urban League formalized this process with employment placement services. The neighborhood's extraordinary density — over 100,000 people in a few square miles — created an intense, layered community life where churches, barbershops, pool halls, and front stoops all functioned as information exchanges.
social_fabric.class_dynamics: The Black elite ('old Detroiters,' pre-1910 arrivals, often light-skinned, Episcopal or Congregational) occupied the top rung. Below them, a substantial middle class emerged from auto industry wages — Ford's $5/day made it possible for a Black man to own a home and join the middle class within a few years of arrival. Below them, newer arrivals and women (who were excluded from the auto plants) worked domestic service, laundry, and the service economy. Class was visible on the street: the old elite attended St. Matthew's Episcopal; the aspiring middle class went to Second Baptist; working-class newcomers founded storefront Holiness and Pentecostal churches that the old guard regarded with suspicion.
social_fabric.gender_roles: The auto industry was overwhelmingly male, creating a gendered economic structure more extreme than other Great Migration destinations. Men's identity centered on factory work — their shift, their plant, their union local. Women worked domestic service, beauty culture, and the informal economy (boarding houses, catering, numbers running). Women also ran the church auxiliaries, managed household finances, and maintained the Southern kin networks that sustained the migration chain. When women did enter industry during World War II, they were concentrated in the most dangerous and lowest-paid positions.
social_fabric.relationship_to_wider_city: Paradise Valley existed because white Detroit violently enforced residential segregation. The Ossian Sweet case (1925), in which a Black doctor was prosecuted for defending his home against a white mob, defined the racial boundary lines. Restrictive covenants covered 80% of Detroit's residential property. Black families who attempted to move outside the approved zone faced arson, bombings, and mob violence. The auto plants were integrated on the shop floor (especially at Ford's River Rouge complex) but the neighborhoods were not. This contradiction — earning a good wage alongside white workers, then returning to an overcrowded ghetto — drove both community solidarity and explosive frustration. The 1943 Detroit race riot, which killed 34 people, erupted from exactly these tensions.

narrative_hooks: When {ancestor_name} stepped off the train at Michigan Central Station around {year}, Detroit was not offering freedom so much as a deal — Ford would pay five dollars a day, the best wage a Black man could earn anywhere in America, but the city would tell {ancestor_name} exactly which streets to live on.

The {surname} family's Detroit began on Hastings Street, in Paradise Valley — a neighborhood so crowded that families slept in shifts, so alive that John Lee Hooker played the blues on one corner while the numbers runners worked the next, and so doomed that the city would bulldoze it all for a freeway within a generation.

If {ancestor_name} worked at Ford's Rouge plant in the {year}s, the walk through the factory gate was one of the few moments in American life where a Black man from {origin_county} stood on something close to equal footing with a white man — and then the shift ended and they went home to different worlds.

By the time the bulldozers came for Black Bottom in the 1950s, the {surname} family had built a life in a neighborhood that white Detroit had always planned to erase — the freeway route through Paradise Valley was no accident, and every church, barbershop, and front porch that fell was someone's anchor.
counter_narratives: Paradise Valley was not simply a slum. It was an overcrowded neighborhood because of enforced segregation, but it contained a thriving commercial district, world-class entertainment venues, Black-owned businesses, and a genuine middle class built on auto industry wages. The 'urban blight' narrative used to justify its destruction was a white planning framework imposed on a living community.

Not all Black Detroiters worked in the auto plants. A substantial service economy — barbershops, beauty parlors, restaurants, nightclubs, funeral homes, insurance agencies — employed thousands. Women, who were largely excluded from factory work until World War II, built their own economic networks in domestic service, beauty culture, and the informal economy.

The destruction of Paradise Valley and Black Bottom by urban renewal and freeway construction (1950s-1960s) was not progress. It scattered a concentrated community across the city, destroyed Black-owned businesses that could not relocate, and severed the institutional networks — churches, clubs, lodges, businesses — that had sustained Black Detroit for forty years. Many genealogical records were lost in the demolition.
source_references: Thomas, Richard W. 'Life for Us Is What We Make It: Building Black Community in Detroit, 1915-1945.' Indiana University Press, 1992.
Sugrue, Thomas J. 'The Origins of the Urban Crisis: Race and Inequality in Postwar Detroit.' Princeton University Press, 1996.
Bjorn, Lars, and Jim Gallert. 'Before Motown: A History of Jazz in Detroit, 1920-60.' University of Michigan Press, 2001.
Moon, Elaine Latzman. 'Untold Tales, Unsung Heroes: An Oral History of Detroit's African American Community, 1918-1967.' Wayne State University Press, 1994.
Burton Historical Collection, Detroit Public Library. (Primary archive for Paradise Valley and Black Bottom institutional records, city directories, photographs, and newspapers.)
